title: United States v. Keith Raniere (NXIVM)
latest_action: Raniere sentenced to 120 years in federal prison.
court: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of New York
summary: Federal prosecution of NXIVM cult leader Keith Raniere for sex trafficking, racketeering, and forced labor. Raniere ran a secret society (DOS) that used blackmail to control women. One of the most high-profile sex trafficking convictions involving an American cult.
description: NXIVM operated as an ostensible self-help organization but was led by Raniere who created DOS ('Dominus Obsequious Sororium'), a secret society that used blackmail material ('collateral') to control female members. Charges included sex trafficking, forced labor, racketeering, identity theft, obstruction of justice. Five co-defendants pleaded guilty including actress Allison Mack and heiress Clare Bronfman. Case received extensive media coverage and raised awareness of coercive control in trafficking.

updates.event: Keith Raniere arrested in Mexico and extradited to US; complaint filed
updates.notes: Arrested following New York Times exposé in October 2017

updates.event: Superseding indictment adding sex trafficking charges
Allison Mack pleads guilty to racketeering charges
updates.notes: Actress known for Smallville — cooperates with prosecution

updates.event: Clare Bronfman pleads guilty to conspiracy charges
updates.notes: Seagram heiress who bankrolled NXIVM

updates.event: Trial begins in Brooklyn federal court
Jury convicts Raniere on all 7 counts — racketeering, sex trafficking, forced labor
updates.notes: Convicted on all counts including sex trafficking of a child

updates.event: Clare Bronfman sentenced to 81 months
Allison Mack sentenced to 36 months
Raniere sentenced to 120 years in federal prison + $3.46M restitution
updates.notes: Raniere maintains innocence; appeals filed

title: United States v. Ghislaine Maxwell
latest_action: Maxwell sentenced to 20 years in federal prison.
court: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
summary: Federal prosecution of British socialite Ghislaine Maxwell for recruiting and grooming underage girls for Jeffrey Epstein's sex trafficking network. Case represented landmark prosecution of a trafficker's recruiter/enabler. Maxwell sentenced to 20 years.
description: Maxwell was charged as the primary recruiter and groomer for Jeffrey Epstein's sex trafficking network, which operated from the mid-1990s through 2004. Charges included sex trafficking of a minor, conspiracy, enticement of a minor, and perjury. Key issues: statute of limitations, victim testimony reliability, and Maxwell's role versus Epstein's (who died in custody in 2019). Case raised questions about wealthy clients who have not been prosecuted. Maxwell appeals conviction claiming juror misconduct.

updates.event: Ghislaine Maxwell arrested in New Hampshire; charged with sex trafficking and enticement
updates.notes: Maxwell had been in hiding for nearly a year following Epstein's death

updates.event: Maxwell denied bail — flight risk; held in MDC Brooklyn
Superseding indictment adding sex trafficking of a minor count
Trial begins in SDNY
Maxwell convicted on 5 of 6 counts including sex trafficking of a minor
updates.notes: Jury deliberated 5 days; acquitted on one enticement count

updates.event: Maxwell sentenced to 20 years in federal prison + $750,000 fine
updates.notes: Maxwell begins serving sentence at FCI Tallahassee

updates.event: Maxwell files appeal arguing juror misconduct tainted trial
updates.notes: Second Circuit appeal pending

updates.event: Second Circuit denies appeal — conviction upheld
updates.actor: U.S. Court of Appeals, Second Circuit
updates.notes: Maxwell remains incarcerated; seeks certiorari to SCOTUS

upcoming.event: Potential Supreme Court cert petition deadline
upcoming.notes: Maxwell's attorneys may petition SCOTUS after Second Circuit denial

title: United States v. Backpage.com (Larkin, Lacey, et al.)
latest_action: Three defendants convicted after second trial. Sentencing hearings ongoing.
court: U.S. District Court, District of Arizona
summary: Federal prosecution of Backpage.com founders and executives for facilitating sex trafficking through their adult advertising platform. First major prosecution of an online platform under trafficking statutes after DOJ seizure of Backpage. Complex litigation over Section 230 and First Amendment defenses.
description: DOJ seized Backpage.com and indicted founders for money laundering and travel act violations related to facilitating prostitution. Case was the first major prosecution testing whether platform operators could be held criminally liable for user-generated trafficking content. Defense raised Section 230 and First Amendment arguments. First trial ended in mistrial in 2023 after prosecution misconduct. Second trial resulted in convictions for 3 of 5 remaining defendants. Case has major implications for platform liability post-FOSTA-SESTA.

updates.event: DOJ seizes Backpage.com — site goes dark
updates.notes: Seizure came one day before FOSTA-SESTA presidential signing

updates.event: Federal indictment filed against Backpage founders (93 counts)
Case proceeds after years of pre-trial motions and Section 230 challenges dismissed
First trial begins; prosecution presents 3+ weeks of evidence
Mistrial declared after prosecution misconduct finding; retrial ordered
updates.notes: Judge found prosecution improperly vouched for witnesses

updates.event: Second trial begins with 5 remaining defendants
Three defendants convicted: Lacey acquitted; Larkin, Spear, Brunst convicted on multiple counts
updates.notes: Convictions on money laundering and Travel Act violations

updates.event: Sentencing hearings begin; delayed multiple times due to defense motions

upcoming.event: Sentencing of remaining convicted defendants expected
upcoming.notes: Larkin and Brunst sentencing; guidelines range 20+ years

title: M.A. v. Wyndham Hotels & Resorts, Inc.
latest_action: Case settled for undisclosed amount. Settlement terms confidential.
court: U.S. District Court, Southern District of Ohio
summary: Landmark sex trafficking lawsuit against major hotel chains under 18 U.S.C. § 1595, alleging hotels knowingly benefited from sex trafficking occurring on their premises. One of the first cases to test hotel liability under the TVPA's civil cause of action. Led to industry-wide training initiatives.
description: Survivor filed under TVPA § 1595 civil cause of action (created by TVPRA 2008), alleging Wyndham and co-defendant hotel chains knowingly benefited from sex trafficking by allowing it to occur at their properties. Key legal theories: (1) Hotels had actual or constructive knowledge of trafficking indicators; (2) Hotels benefited financially from harboring traffickers; (3) Hotels failed to train staff on trafficking identification despite widespread awareness. Case part of a wave of TVPA hotel liability litigation.

updates.event: Complaint filed against 4 hotel chains under TVPA § 1595
updates.actor: Plaintiff M.A. (via Thorn / trafficking survivor advocates)
updates.notes: First major TVPA hotel liability case in Ohio federal court

updates.event: Hotel chains file motions to dismiss — argue no actual knowledge of trafficking
MTD denied as to Wyndham and Marriott; discovery proceeds
updates.notes: Court holds § 1595 'benefit' standard does not require actual knowledge

updates.event: AH&LA (hotel trade association) files amicus brief supporting hotel chains
Hotel industry launches 'No Room for Trafficking' training program amid litigation pressure
updates.notes: 100,000+ employees trained in hospitality sector

updates.event: All cases settled for undisclosed amounts; plaintiff receives confidential settlement
updates.notes: Terms confidential; hotels deny liability

title: Jane Doe (34 plaintiffs) v. MindGeek USA Inc. (Pornhub)
latest_action: Class certification briefing completed. MindGeek acquired by Ethical Capital Partners; renamed Aylo. Discovery continues.
court: U.S. District Court, Central District of California
summary: Class action against Pornhub's parent company MindGeek and payment processors Visa and Mastercard for knowingly hosting non-consensual, trafficking-related content. Cases prompted Pornhub to remove millions of unverified videos. Visa and Mastercard suspensions of Pornhub payment processing in 2020 led to mass content removal.
description: Lawsuits allege MindGeek knowingly profited from CSAM, non-consensual content, and trafficking-related material on Pornhub. Visa and Mastercard named as defendants for processing payments knowing the platform hosted such content. Landmark because it extends trafficking liability to financial institutions. MindGeek sold site and rebranded as Aylo. New York Times investigation in Dec 2020 preceded credit card suspensions and mass content removal. Cases rely on TVPA § 1591 and § 1595, as well as Racketeering Act.

updates.event: New York Times exposé 'The Children of Pornhub' published
updates.notes: Catalyzing event for litigation and platform changes

updates.event: Visa and Mastercard suspend payments to Pornhub
updates.notes: Pornhub removes 10.4 million unverified videos following card suspensions

updates.event: Complaint filed — 34 John/Jane Does vs. MindGeek and payment processors
MindGeek MTD denied as to § 1595 beneficiary theory; Visa and Mastercard motions taken under advisement
Court allows Visa and Mastercard to remain as defendants — first case holding payment processors may face trafficking liability
updates.notes: Significant expansion of § 1595 liability theory

updates.event: MindGeek/Aylo files for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in Canada
updates.notes: Complicates US litigation — automatic stay issues

updates.event: Class certification briefing completed; court to rule in 2025

upcoming.notes: If certified, class could include thousands of trafficking and CSAM victims

upcoming.event: Trial date if class not certified; individual bellwether cases proceed

title: Jane Does v. Salesforce, Inc.
latest_action: Seventh Circuit reversed district court dismissal; remanded for discovery.
court: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Illinois / Seventh Circuit
judge: Judge Matthew Kennelly (District); Seventh Circuit panel
summary: TVPA civil suit alleging Salesforce provided CRM software and marketing tools to Backpage.com knowing it was used to facilitate sex trafficking. If successful, would establish that technology vendors can face trafficking liability for tools they sell to platforms engaged in trafficking.
description: Plaintiffs argue Salesforce knowingly benefited from providing CRM and marketing automation software to Backpage.com, which used these tools to manage its trafficking-facilitated adult advertising. Key legal issue: whether a technology vendor that provides tools (not content) can be a 'beneficiary' under TVPA § 1595. District court dismissed; Seventh Circuit reversed, holding plaintiffs plausibly alleged Salesforce knew of Backpage's trafficking nexus. Case extends § 1595 liability theory significantly beyond platforms and hotels to software vendors.

updates.event: Complaint filed in N.D. Illinois against Salesforce
District court grants Salesforce motion to dismiss — holds no § 1595 liability for software vendors
Plaintiffs appeal to Seventh Circuit
Seventh Circuit reverses dismissal — holds § 1595 can reach software vendors; remands for discovery
updates.actor: U.S. Court of Appeals, Seventh Circuit
updates.notes: Major expansion of § 1595 liability; tech industry closely watching

upcoming.event: Discovery deadline in district court on remand
upcoming.notes: Plaintiffs need to show Salesforce had actual knowledge of trafficking use

upcoming.event: Summary judgment motions expected following discovery

title: John Does 1–6 v. Twitter, Inc.
latest_action: Case settled for undisclosed amount following Ninth Circuit reversal.
court: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California / Ninth Circuit
summary: CSAM and trafficking lawsuit against Twitter alleging the platform failed to remove CSAM depicting plaintiffs after being notified, and profited from hosting the content. Ninth Circuit reversed initial dismissal, holding FOSTA-SESTA's § 230 carve-out applied. Settlement followed reversal.
description: Minors alleged they were solicited and their images exploited on Twitter, with Twitter refusing to remove content despite reports. Legal issue centered on whether FOSTA-SESTA's carve-out to Section 230 immunity applied, or whether Twitter retained immunity. Ninth Circuit held that at the motion to dismiss stage, plaintiffs plausibly alleged Twitter 'knowingly benefited' from CSAM under TVPA. Case settled before trial. Twitter (now X) has faced ongoing CSAM enforcement criticism.

updates.event: Complaint filed in N.D. Cal. against Twitter under TVPA §§ 1591/1595
District court dismisses — holds § 230 bars claims; FOSTA-SESTA carve-out inapplicable
Plaintiffs appeal to Ninth Circuit
Ninth Circuit reverses dismissal — holds FOSTA-SESTA carve-out to § 230 may apply; remands
updates.actor: U.S. Court of Appeals, Ninth Circuit
updates.notes: Significant ruling expanding potential platform liability under FOSTA-SESTA

updates.event: Case settled for undisclosed amount before retrial
updates.notes: Twitter denies wrongdoing; terms confidential

title: Jane Doe v. Snap Inc. (Snapchat)
latest_action: MDL proceedings ongoing in C.D. California. Cases consolidated under Judge Dolly Gee.
court: U.S. District Court, Central District of California (MDL)
summary: Consolidated MDL against Snapchat alleging the platform's design features (disappearing messages, minimal content moderation) enabled grooming and sex trafficking of minors. Claims include negligence, product liability, and TVPA violations. Part of broader wave of social media child safety litigation.
description: Multiple lawsuits consolidated against Snap Inc. alleging Snapchat's disappearing-message feature enabled traffickers and groomers to communicate with minors without a record. Plaintiffs allege Snap's product design choices were negligent and violated COPPA. Unlike Section 230 defenses that protect against editorial liability, plaintiffs frame claims around product design defects. Part of the same litigation wave as Meta and TikTok social media child safety MDLs.

updates.event: First complaints filed against Snap
JPML consolidates Snap cases into MDL in C.D. California
30+ state AGs file multistate action against Snap; coordinate with MDL
Discovery ongoing; bellwether trial selection process underway

title: EEOC v. Global Horizons, Inc. (Labor Trafficking in Agriculture)
latest_action: Consent decree entered. Global Horizons paid $7.65 million to 200+ Thai workers.
court: U.S. District Court, District of Hawaii
summary: EEOC labor trafficking lawsuit against agricultural labor contractor Global Horizons for subjecting Thai guest workers to forced labor through debt bondage, confiscation of passports, and threats of deportation. One of the largest farm labor trafficking settlements in US history.
description: Global Horizons recruited hundreds of Thai workers with promises of legal agricultural work in the US under H-2A visas. Upon arrival, workers were subjected to: forced labor through debt bondage (excessive recruitment fees); confiscation of passports and travel documents; threats of deportation for noncompliance; substandard housing and food withheld as leverage; and psychological coercion. Settlement included $7.65M in damages for 200+ workers and significant remedial measures for future H-2A recruitment.

updates.event: Federal trafficking charges filed against Global Horizons executives
updates.notes: Criminal charges preceded civil EEOC action

updates.event: EEOC files civil complaint in D. Hawaii
Consent decree entered — $7.65M settlement for 200+ Thai workers
updates.notes: One of the largest agricultural labor trafficking settlements in US history

title: Doe v. Nestlé USA, Inc. (Cocoa Supply Chain Forced Labor)
latest_action: Supreme Court unanimously reversed; case remanded to district court. Still active after 19 years.
court: U.S. District Court, Central District of California; Supreme Court of the United States
plaintiffs: John Doe I–VI (former child slaves from Mali)
summary: 20-year Alien Tort Statute case alleging Nestlé and Cargill aided and abetted forced child labor in Côte d'Ivoire cocoa supply chains. Supreme Court held federal courts lack ATS jurisdiction over foreign conduct, but left open whether domestic conduct allegations could proceed. Landmark case for corporate supply chain liability.
description: Malian children were trafficked and forced to work on cocoa farms in Côte d'Ivoire supplying Nestlé and Cargill. Plaintiffs sued under the Alien Tort Statute for aiding and abetting forced labor. After 16 years of litigation, the Supreme Court (9-0) held that foreign conduct cannot support ATS jurisdiction; remanded on question of whether US-based decisions suffice. Case illustrates extraordinary difficulty of supply chain forced labor litigation and the 20+ year timeframes involved.

updates.event: ATS complaint filed in C.D. California
District court dismisses — lack of ATS jurisdiction for foreign conduct
Ninth Circuit partially reverses — allows case to proceed
updates.actor: U.S. Court of Appeals, Ninth Circuit

updates.event: Ninth Circuit (en banc) reverses again — full case dismissed
Supreme Court reverses and remands (9-0) — Justice Thomas writes; ATS jurisdictional question partly resolved
updates.actor: Supreme Court of the United States
updates.notes: Nestle v. Doe, 593 U.S. 369 (2021)

updates.event: Case remanded; district court proceedings resume
Case remains active on remand; summary judgment briefing ongoing

title: Hotel Industry TVPA Liability Cases — Nationwide Wave (2019–present)
latest_action: 350+ cases filed nationwide. S.D. Ohio serving as de facto leading jurisdiction. Cases against Red Roof Inn, Super 8, Comfort Inn, and others proceeding to trial.
summary: Wave of TVPA § 1595 civil suits filed against major hotel chains and franchisors alleging they knowingly benefited from sex trafficking occurring on their premises. 350+ cases filed nationwide. Key legal issues: whether franchisor can be liable for franchisee properties; what constitutes 'knowledge' of trafficking.
description: Since 2019, hundreds of sex trafficking survivors have filed TVPA § 1595 civil suits against hotels. Key issues being litigated: (1) Franchisor liability for trafficking at franchised properties; (2) Standards for 'constructive knowledge' of trafficking; (3) Role of AH&LA 'No Room for Trafficking' training program; (4) Class certification viability; (5) Expert testimony on trafficking indicators. Several cases have resulted in seven-figure settlements. Industry has spent $100M+ on defense and training in response.

updates.event: First wave of TVPA hotel cases filed in S.D. Ohio following M.A. v. Wyndham precedent
AH&LA launches mandatory 'No Room for Trafficking' staff training program
350+ cases filed nationwide; courts begin coordinating discovery
First bellwether trials scheduled in S.D. Ohio; discovery ongoing
Multiple seven-figure settlements; Red Roof Inns settles 13 cases for undisclosed amounts
Trials ongoing; franchisor liability theory being tested at trial for first time

upcoming.notes: Choice Hotels International case going to trial in S.D. Ohio

title: U.S. Territories & Civil Suits v. Jeffrey Epstein Estate / JP Morgan / Deutsche Bank
latest_action: JP Morgan settled for $75M with US Virgin Islands and $290M with Epstein victims. Deutsche Bank settled for $75M. Ongoing civil actions against Epstein estate.
court: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
judge: Judge Jed Rakoff (JP Morgan); Judge Loretta Preska
summary: Post-Epstein death civil litigation targeting his financial enablers: JP Morgan Chase and Deutsche Bank are accused of facilitating Epstein's trafficking enterprise by processing his finances while knowing of his criminal conduct. JP Morgan settled for $290M to victims; Deutsche Bank settled for $75M. Landmark corporate enabler liability cases.
description: Jeffrey Epstein died in federal custody in August 2019 while awaiting trial on sex trafficking charges. Civil litigation subsequently targeted financial institutions that serviced his accounts while allegedly knowing of his trafficking. US Virgin Islands government sued JP Morgan, which settled for $75M with USVI and $290M with victim class. Deutsche Bank settled for $75M. Cases establish that financial institutions can face TVPA civil liability for knowingly processing transactions related to trafficking.

updates.event: Jeffrey Epstein found dead in federal custody at MCC New York
updates.notes: Death ruled suicide; extensive conspiracy theories follow

updates.event: US Virgin Islands sues JP Morgan and Deutsche Bank for facilitating Epstein's enterprise
JP Morgan settles with USVI for $75M
Deutsche Bank agrees to pay $75M settlement to Epstein victims
JP Morgan agrees to $290M settlement to Epstein victim class
updates.notes: Largest Epstein-related financial institution settlement

updates.event: Continued civil actions against Epstein estate ongoing; individual victim suits active

title: A.B. v. Marriott International, Inc.
allegations: Marriott hotel near Philadelphia Airport allowed sex trafficking of plaintiff (then 22) at three properties (2009–2011); ignored signs of trafficking visible to staff
knowingly_benefit: MTD denied Apr 22, 2020; 'knew or should have known' sufficient at pleading
legal_significance: First major hotel TVPA suit in 3rd Circuit; established 'knew or should have known' pleading standard
specific_metrics: Plaintiff alleges staff observed cash payments, multiple men visiting room; no calls to front desk; no record of local check-in
summary: First major hotel TVPA suit in 3rd Circuit; established 'knew or should have known' pleading standard
description: Marriott hotel near Philadelphia Airport allowed sex trafficking of plaintiff (then 22) at three properties (2009–2011); ignored signs of trafficking visible to staff Active case; part of wave of 200+ new hotel trafficking lawsuits filed in 2025 alone

title: Doe v. Red Roof Inns, Inc.
allegations: Red Roof Inn properties in Atlanta metro area allowed repeated sex trafficking; management and staff observed but ignored visible indicators; franchise system profited from room revenue
knowingly_benefit: 11th Circuit: narrowest reading - requires plaintiff to allege defendant participated in common undertaking with specific trafficker AND knew of that plaintiff's trafficking specifically
settlement_amount: Confidential mid-trial settlement (Atlanta, 2022); $40 million jury verdict (separate Georgia motel case, first jury verdict under TVPA against hotel)
survivor_compensation: Partial compensation for settling plaintiffs; jury award not yet collected
resolution: Operational changes required at settled properties
legal_significance: Doe v. Red Roof Inns 21 F.4th 714 (11th Cir. 2021) = NARROWEST §1595 circuit standard. SCOTUS cert denied (No. 21-7234, Feb 2022). Creates circuit split vs. 7th Circuit Salesforce ruling
specific_metrics: $40M verdict: $10M compensatory + $30M punitive. 'Narrowest circuit' approach still allows some hotel cases to survive.
summary: Doe v. Red Roof Inns 21 F.4th 714 (11th Cir. 2021) = NARROWEST §1595 circuit standard. SCOTUS cert denied (No. 21-7234, Feb 2022). Creates circuit split vs. 7th Circuit Salesforce ruling
description: Red Roof Inn properties in Atlanta metro area allowed repeated sex trafficking; management and staff observed but ignored visible indicators; franchise system profited from room revenue Most significant hotel trafficking verdict; 11th Circuit standard now in tension with 7th Circuit Salesforce standard

updates.event: Complaint filed — Doe v. Red Roof Inns, Inc.
Status as of March 2026: Partially settled (Atlanta); other cases active
updates.notes: Most significant hotel trafficking verdict; 11th Circuit standard now in tension with 7th Circuit Salesforce standard

title: A.M.G. v. Red Roof Inns, Inc.
allegations: Trafficking at Red Roof Inn properties in Ohio; plaintiff claims hotel staff directly facilitated by providing rooms knowing they were used for sex trafficking
knowingly_benefit: S.D. Ohio: franchisor cannot simply shift §1595 liability to franchisees; franchisor's knowledge + financial benefit from franchise fees can suffice
legal_significance: September 30, 2025 ruling: FRANCHISORS cannot escape §1595 by delegating to franchisees; deepens circuit split on franchisor liability
specific_metrics: Ohio ruling directly conflicts with 11th Circuit narrower approach; creates important precedent on franchisor liability structure for national hotel chains
summary: September 30, 2025 ruling: FRANCHISORS cannot escape §1595 by delegating to franchisees; deepens circuit split on franchisor liability
description: Trafficking at Red Roof Inn properties in Ohio; plaintiff claims hotel staff directly facilitated by providing rooms knowing they were used for sex trafficking Key test of whether national hotel brands (Marriott, Hilton, Wyndham, Choice Hotels) can be held liable even if individual franchise operators are responsible

updates.event: Complaint filed — A.M.G. v. Red Roof Inns, Inc.
Status as of March 2026: Active
updates.notes: Key test of whether national hotel brands (Marriott, Hilton, Wyndham, Choice Hotels) can be held liable even if individual franchise operators are responsible

title: Hotel Industry Sex Trafficking Litigation MDL No. 3104
defendants: Multiple hotel chains: Marriott, Hilton, Wyndham, Choice Hotels, G6 Hospitality (Motel 6), Super 8
allegations: Multiple hotel chains knew or should have known their properties were used for sex trafficking; ignored warning signs; profited from room revenue
knowingly_benefit: JPML denied consolidation April 2024 (previously denied MDL 2928 in Jan 2020) — cases remain in individual district courts
settlement_amount: Various — $40M verdict (GA); $24M (Philadelphia Days Inn, 8 plaintiffs); $17.5M settlement (3 Philadelphia hotels, March 2025); $37.5M and $24.5M arbitration awards; $6M (Super 8 GA); $5M (Days Inn/Wyndham, Stockbridge GA, Sept 2025)
survivor_compensation: Yes — compensatory damages to survivor-plaintiffs in all verdicts/settlements
legal_significance: JPML twice denied consolidation, finding hotel cases too fact-specific for MDL treatment. This keeps litigation fragmented but also means survivors must pursue individually — access to justice concern.
specific_metrics: 200+ new TVPA hotel lawsuits filed in 2025 alone. Total §1595 hotel cases = largest single category in HT civil litigation.

title: United States v. Lacey, Brunst, Spear et al.
defendants: Michael Lacey (co-founder), John 'Jed' Brunst (CFO), Scott Spear (COO) + others
allegations: Lacey, Brunst, Spear ran Backpage.com which earned >$500M facilitating sex trafficking through adult ads; Backpage earned $135M in 2014 alone; 90%+ of revenue from adult ads; concealed money through cryptocurrency and foreign banks
knowingly_benefit: All three convicted November 2023: Lacey (5 yrs prison + $3M fine); Spear (10 yrs); Brunst (10 yrs). All ordered to surrender Sept 11, 2024.
settlement_amount: $215 million civil forfeiture settlement (Dec 2024, CV 18-8420 RGK, C.D. Cal.)
survivor_compensation: Yes - Backpage Compensation Program launched mid-2025 (BackpageRemission.com); filing deadline March 31, 2026; administered by Epiq Global Inc.; >$200M available for victim remission
resolution: Backpage.com shut down April 2018 by federal seizure
legal_significance: First major prosecution of an online platform for facilitating sex trafficking. Demonstrated that platform operators can be criminally liable when they knowingly facilitate trafficking.
specific_metrics: Backpage was used in 73% of sex trafficking cases reported to NCMEC in 2017. FBI and DOJ coordinated multi-year investigation.
summary: First major prosecution of an online platform for facilitating sex trafficking. Demonstrated that platform operators can be criminally liable when they knowingly facilitate trafficking.
description: Lacey, Brunst, Spear ran Backpage.com which earned >$500M facilitating sex trafficking through adult ads; Backpage earned $135M in 2014 alone; 90%+ of revenue from adult ads; concealed money through cryptocurrency and foreign banks DOJ announcing victim compensation process (BackpageRemission.com) is noteworthy - rare example of federal government creating survivor compensation from criminal forfeiture
companies: Michael Lacey (co-founder), John 'Jed' Brunst (CFO), Scott Spear (COO) + others

updates.actor: Michael Lacey (co-founder), John 'Jed' Brunst (CFO), Scott Spear (COO) + others

title: G.G. v. Salesforce.com, Inc.
status_detail: Active - TRIAL SET EARLY JUNE 2026 (potentially first Salesforce trafficking case to reach jury)
latest_action: Active - TRIAL SET EARLY JUNE 2026 (potentially first Salesforce trafficking case to reach jury)
court: N.D. Illinois; 7th Circuit Court of Appeals
allegations: Salesforce provided CRM and marketing tools to Backpage knowing it was used for sex trafficking; Salesforce's software managed Backpage's global sales operations; Salesforce employees specifically flagged Backpage's business to internal leadership as problematic
knowingly_benefit: 7th Circuit August 2023 (76 F.4th 544): BROADEST §1595 ruling - 'venture' need not be a sex-trafficking venture; no constructive knowledge of specific victim required; CDA §230 does NOT shield Salesfo
survivor_compensation: TBD - potentially significant damages if plaintiff prevails
legal_significance: MOST SIGNIFICANT §1595 CASE NOW: Directly conflicts with 11th Circuit's narrow Red Roof Inns standard. 7th Circuit's broad reading: any commercial venture + known benefit suffices. Circuit split makes SCOTUS review increasingly likely after trial.
specific_metrics: 5th Circuit parallel cases (A.B. v. Salesforce, N.D. Tex., 25 cases): partially survived MTD March 2025, following different analysis than 7th Circuit
summary: MOST SIGNIFICANT §1595 CASE NOW: Directly conflicts with 11th Circuit's narrow Red Roof Inns standard. 7th Circuit's broad reading: any commercial venture + known benefit suffices. Circuit split makes SCOTUS review increasingly likely after trial.
description: Salesforce provided CRM and marketing tools to Backpage knowing it was used for sex trafficking; Salesforce's software managed Backpage's global sales operations; Salesforce employees specifically flagged Backpage's business to internal leadership as problematic The §1595 circuit split between 7th (Salesforce/broad) and 11th (Red Roof/narrow) is the most critical pending legal question in HT civil litigation as of March 2026

title: Serena Fleites v. MindGeek S.A.R.L. et al.
allegations: Fleites was 13 when boyfriend posted video on Pornhub titled '13-Year Old Brunette Shows Off For the Camera' - video garnered 400,000+ views; MindGeek refused to remove despite repeated requests; Visa provided payment processing knowing of widespread CSAM
knowingly_benefit: LANDMARK July 2022 ruling: Visa's motion to dismiss DENIED - court found Visa 'lent to MindGeek a much-needed tool - its payment network - with alleged knowledge there was a wealth of monetized child
survivor_compensation: TBD - Fleites case; multiple Jane Doe plaintiffs
legal_significance: First ruling establishing payment processor liability under TVPRA. Payment processor Visa cannot claim ignorance of platform's CSAM when it continued providing services.
specific_metrics: 400,000+ views of video of 13-year-old; MindGeek earned advertising revenue on CSAM content; Visa processed payments for MindGeek subscriptions; MindGeek's 'notice-and-takedown' system failed repeatedly
summary: First ruling establishing payment processor liability under TVPRA. Payment processor Visa cannot claim ignorance of platform's CSAM when it continued providing services.
description: Fleites was 13 when boyfriend posted video on Pornhub titled '13-Year Old Brunette Shows Off For the Camera' - video garnered 400,000+ views; MindGeek refused to remove despite repeated requests; Visa provided payment processing knowing of widespread CSAM Critical precedent: banks and payment processors may face §1595 liability if they knowingly continue processing payments for platforms with widespread CSAM/trafficking content

updates.event: Complaint filed — Serena Fleites v. MindGeek S.A.R.L. et al.
Status as of March 2026: Active - settlement negotiations began Nov 2024
updates.notes: Critical precedent: banks and payment processors may face §1595 liability if they knowingly continue processing payments for platforms with widespread CSAM/trafficking content

title: Doe #1 v. MG Freesites LTD (MindGeek class action)
plaintiffs: Class of minors who appeared in videos on MindGeek websites
allegations: MindGeek/Pornhub systematically hosted CSAM; content moderation systems were inadequate by design; the company monetized content featuring minors
knowingly_benefit: Class CERTIFIED: all persons under 18 when appearing in video/image on any MindGeek website from Feb 12, 2011 to present
legal_significance: First certified class action against a major pornography platform under TVPRA. Class certification is major milestone — creates potential for significant class-wide damages.
specific_metrics: Canadian parallel class action seeks $600 million CAD. Jane Does 1-50 v. MindGeek (S.D. Cal.) settled Oct 2021 for confidential amount — those GirlsDoPorn videos had 700 million combined views.
summary: First certified class action against a major pornography platform under TVPRA. Class certification is major milestone — creates potential for significant class-wide damages.
description: MindGeek/Pornhub systematically hosted CSAM; content moderation systems were inadequate by design; the company monetized content featuring minors Lead counsel: Levin Papantonio and Susman Godfrey

title: Epstein Victims' Compensation Program
defendants: Estate of Jeffrey Epstein (valued $650M+ at death)
legal_provisions: N/A — private compensation program, not court-ordered
allegations: Epstein systematically recruited and trafficked young women and girls, primarily at Palm Beach FL and Little St. James USVI properties; used money and gifts to maintain control
knowingly_benefit: Yes — program established; $121M+ disbursed to 135 claimants
settlement_amount: $121 million+ disbursed (closed Aug 2021); 225 applications; ~150 eligible; 92% accepted; average payout approx. $900K
survivor_compensation: Direct cash compensation to survivors — most comprehensive survivor compensation in any trafficking case to date
legal_significance: Largest per-survivor compensation in trafficking history. Estate also settled separately with USVI for $105M (Dec 2022). JPMorgan paid $290M. Deutsche Bank paid $75M. Bank of America settling Mar 2026.
specific_metrics: 225 survivors submitted claims; 135 paid. Program administrator reported no formula used — individualized assessment of harm.
summary: Largest per-survivor compensation in trafficking history. Estate also settled separately with USVI for $105M (Dec 2022). JPMorgan paid $290M. Deutsche Bank paid $75M. Bank of America settling Mar 2026.
description: Epstein systematically recruited and trafficked young women and girls, primarily at Palm Beach FL and Little St. James USVI properties; used money and gifts to maintain control Aggregate Epstein-related compensation across all defendants: $626M+ and growing
companies: Estate of Jeffrey Epstein (valued $650M+ at death)

title: Jane Doe 1 v. JPMorgan Chase & Co.
allegations: JPMorgan Chase maintained Epstein's accounts for 15+ years after his 2008 sex crime conviction; processed $1 billion+ in payments including payments to young women described as 'masseuses'; received internal red flags from compliance and ignored them; Jamie Dimon personally involved in Epstein relat
knowingly_benefit: $290M settlement June 2023. Prior to settlement, Judge Rakoff found evidence 'JPMorgan treated Epstein as a prized client even as his sex trafficking of young girls and women became public knowledge'
settlement_amount: $290 million — largest §1595 bank settlement in history
survivor_compensation: Yes  class-wide compensation to Epstein trafficking survivors (distribution terms under seal)
resolution: JPMorgan agreed to cooperate with enforcement and implement anti-human-trafficking controls
legal_significance: Established that major financial institutions can face §1595 liability for knowingly providing banking services to sex traffickers. First billion-dollar-scale bank settlement in HT litigation.
specific_metrics: JPMorgan banked Epstein Jan 1998–Dec 2013; processed $1B+ through accounts; compliance flagged Epstein 2007-2013; Jes Staley (future Barclays CEO) was JPMorgan's Epstein relationship manager
summary: Established that major financial institutions can face §1595 liability for knowingly providing banking services to sex traffickers. First billion-dollar-scale bank settlement in HT litigation.
description: JPMorgan Chase maintained Epstein's accounts for 15+ years after his 2008 sex crime conviction; processed $1 billion+ in payments including payments to young women described as 'masseuses'; received internal red flags from compliance and ignored them; Jamie Dimon personally involved in Epstein relationship Counsel: Boies Schiller Flexner for plaintiff. Settlement class period: Jan 1, 1998 – Aug 10, 2019.

resolution: Deutsche Bank improved anti-trafficking compliance protocols

summary: Companion case to JPMorgan. Together = first time major banks faced class action §1595 liability. Deutsche Bank took on a known sex offender to poach business from JPMorgan.

title: Doe v. Apple Inc., et al. (Congo cobalt mining)
allegations: Tech companies knowingly benefited from forced child labor in DRC cobalt mines (used in batteries for products they sold); companies had audit reports confirming child labor but continued purchasing cobalt from same supply chain
knowingly_benefit: D.C. Circuit affirmed dismissal March 5, 2024: purchasing cobalt through global supply chain does NOT constitute 'participation in a venture' under TVPRA. Narrowest supply chain reading yet.
legal_significance: SIGNIFICANT NEGATIVE PRECEDENT: D.C. Circuit severely limits TVPRA supply chain liability. Together with Nestlé v. Doe (ATS), companies can purchase from forced-labor supply chains with limited U.S. federal civil liability.
specific_metrics: Plaintiffs claimed children as young as 6 died or were injured in cobalt mines; companies had knowledge through audits and investigative reporting (Washington Post 2016 report) but continued practices
summary: SIGNIFICANT NEGATIVE PRECEDENT: D.C. Circuit severely limits TVPRA supply chain liability. Together with Nestlé v. Doe (ATS), companies can purchase from forced-labor supply chains with limited U.S. federal civil liability.
description: Tech companies knowingly benefited from forced child labor in DRC cobalt mines (used in batteries for products they sold); companies had audit reports confirming child labor but continued purchasing cobalt from same supply chain Three circuits now have divergent TVPRA supply chain standards; SCOTUS has not resolved the question

updates.event: Complaint filed — Doe v. Apple Inc., et al. (Congo cobalt mining)
Status as of March 2026: Dismissed March 5, 2024
updates.notes: Three circuits now have divergent TVPRA supply chain standards; SCOTUS has not resolved the question

title: Nestlé USA Inc. v. Doe / Cargill Inc. v. Doe
status_detail: Dismissed (ATS claims); remanded for TVPA analysis
latest_action: Dismissed (ATS claims); remanded for TVPA analysis
plaintiffs: Six Malian citizens trafficked as child laborers on Ivory Coast cocoa farms
allegations: Nestlé and Cargill purchased cocoa from Ivory Coast farms using child labor; had direct ties to Côte d'Ivoire operations (providing technical/financial assistance to farms); profit from child slave labor
knowingly_benefit: SCOTUS held 8-1 (Thomas, J.): ATS claims sought impermissible extraterritorial application. U.S. domestic conduct insufficient. Did NOT resolve whether corporations can be sued under ATS.
legal_significance: SCOTUS held U.S. corporations cannot be sued under ATS for foreign child labor, even if their domestic headquarters made policy decisions enabling that labor. TVPA claims not fully resolved — case remanded.
specific_metrics: Cocoa supply chain: Nestlé provides technical/financial assistance to Ivory Coast farms, purchased their cocoa for KitKat/Butterfinger, etc. Plaintiffs trafficked as children on those farms.
summary: SCOTUS held U.S. corporations cannot be sued under ATS for foreign child labor, even if their domestic headquarters made policy decisions enabling that labor. TVPA claims not fully resolved — case remanded.
description: Nestlé and Cargill purchased cocoa from Ivory Coast farms using child labor; had direct ties to Côte d'Ivoire operations (providing technical/financial assistance to farms); profit from child slave labor Post-Nestlé, plaintiffs are redirected to TVPA §§1595-1596 for supply chain claims; ATS avenue largely closed. See also Doe v. Apple (D.C. Cir. 2024) which further limits supply chain TVPA claims.

updates.event: Complaint filed — Nestlé USA Inc. v. Doe / Cargill Inc. v. Doe
Status as of March 2026: Dismissed (ATS claims); remanded for TVPA analysis
updates.notes: Post-Nestlé, plaintiffs are redirected to TVPA §§1595-1596 for supply chain claims; ATS avenue largely closed. See also Doe v. Apple (D.C. Cir. 2024) which further limits supply chain TVPA claims.

title: Ratha et al. v. Phatthana Seafood Co.
status_detail: Dismissed (9th Cir.); SCOTUS cert denied Dec 2022
latest_action: Dismissed (9th Cir.); SCOTUS cert denied Dec 2022
plaintiffs: 7 Cambodian plaintiffs (trafficked into Thai seafood processing)
allegations: Cambodian villagers deceived into working in Thai shrimp processing plants under threat and coercion; Rubicon Resources imported and sold the shrimp (to Walmart, Costco) knowing it was produced with forced labor
knowingly_benefit: 9th Circuit Feb 2022: Rubicon did NOT 'knowingly benefit' because Walmart rejected the tainted shipment (no completed benefit). Congress responded by amending TVPRA to include 'attempt to benefit'.
legal_significance: SIGNIFICANT: Congress directly amended TVPRA (in 2023 reauthorization) to correct 9th Circuit's 'completed benefit only' requirement — now includes those who 'attempt or conspire to benefit'. Shows litigation shapes legislation.
specific_metrics: Ratha is the landmark case establishing that Congress will intervene when courts narrow TVPRA too much. Thai seafood supply chains supply Walmart, Costco, Whole Foods.
summary: SIGNIFICANT: Congress directly amended TVPRA (in 2023 reauthorization) to correct 9th Circuit's 'completed benefit only' requirement — now includes those who 'attempt or conspire to benefit'. Shows litigation shapes legislation.
description: Cambodian villagers deceived into working in Thai shrimp processing plants under threat and coercion; Rubicon Resources imported and sold the shrimp (to Walmart, Costco) knowing it was produced with forced labor Post-amendment: new supply chain cases can proceed even if buyer rejected tainted goods; 'attempt to benefit' now sufficient

updates.event: Complaint filed — Ratha et al. v. Phatthana Seafood Co.
Status as of March 2026: Dismissed (9th Cir.); SCOTUS cert denied Dec 2022
updates.notes: Post-amendment: new supply chain cases can proceed even if buyer rejected tainted goods; 'attempt to benefit' now sufficient

title: United States v. Sean Combs
defendants: Sean Combs (a/k/a Puff Daddy, Diddy, Brother Love)
allegations: Combs allegedly organized 'freak offs' (forced sexual performances) using sex workers under psychological and physical coercion; used associates to recruit, control, and silence victims; RICO enterprise operated through Bad Boy Entertainment
knowingly_benefit: Guilty on 2 counts: transportation for prostitution (§2421). NOT GUILTY: racketeering, sex trafficking. Verdict July 2, 2025.
legal_significance: First major celebrity criminal trafficking conviction. Outcome (acquittal on RICO/trafficking but conviction on prostitution transportation) reflects challenges of proving §1591 'force/fraud/coercion' beyond reasonable doubt.
specific_metrics: FBI seized Combs' homes March 2024; videotape of assault on Cassie Ventura at Intercontinental Hotel 2016 released by CNN. Cassie sued and settled for $20M the day after filing (Nov 2023).
summary: First major celebrity criminal trafficking conviction. Outcome (acquittal on RICO/trafficking but conviction on prostitution transportation) reflects challenges of proving §1591 'force/fraud/coercion' beyond reasonable doubt.
description: Combs allegedly organized 'freak offs' (forced sexual performances) using sex workers under psychological and physical coercion; used associates to recruit, control, and silence victims; RICO enterprise operated through Bad Boy Entertainment Criminal verdict July 2025; sentencing pending as of March 2026; 4 years 2 months sentenced.
companies: Sean Combs (a/k/a Puff Daddy, Diddy, Brother Love)

updates.notes: Criminal verdict July 2025; sentencing pending as of March 2026; 4 years 2 months sentenced.

knowingly_benefit: §1595 TVPA claims against Combs personally survived MTD (March 2025); dismissed against corporate entity Bad Boy Entertainment. Attorney Tony Buzbee is lead plaintiff counsel for 120+ plaintiffs.
settlement_amount: Cassie Ventura settlement: $20 million (day-after-filing settlement, Nov 2023); other individual settlements pending; class terms TBD
resolution: TBD — some plaintiffs seeking injunctive relief from corporate entities
legal_significance: Largest celebrity trafficking civil litigation in history by plaintiff count. §1595 claims allowed against Combs personally = important for future celebrity trafficking cases.

summary: Largest celebrity trafficking civil litigation in history by plaintiff count. §1595 claims allowed against Combs personally = important for future celebrity trafficking cases.

title: Menocal et al. v. GEO Group, Inc.
plaintiffs: Class of 60,000+ ICE detainees at Aurora, CO facility
defendants: The GEO Group, Inc. (private prison company)
legal_provisions: TVPA §1589 (forced labor); Colorado Minimum Wage Act; Unjust Enrichment
allegations: GEO Group forced ICE detainees to clean facility for $1/day (or face solitary confinement) — classic forced labor coercion; GEO profited from labor while paying well below minimum wage
knowingly_benefit: Class certified: 60,000+ detainees. 10th Circuit upheld certification. SCOTUS declined cert October 2018. Forced labor coercion established by threat of punitive action (solitary).
survivor_compensation: Yes — wage compensation and damages for forced labor
resolution: Potential injunctive relief against GEO Group labor practices
legal_significance: LANDMARK: First class certification of detained immigrants for forced labor under TVPA. Establishes that immigration detention ≠ consent to labor.
specific_metrics: $1/day wages; threat of solitary confinement for refusal; GEO earned millions from detainee labor while federal contract paid $30+/day per detainee for housing
summary: LANDMARK: First class certification of detained immigrants for forced labor under TVPA. Establishes that immigration detention ≠ consent to labor.
description: GEO Group forced ICE detainees to clean facility for $1/day (or face solitary confinement) — classic forced labor coercion; GEO profited from labor while paying well below minimum wage GEO is world's largest private prison company ($2B+ revenue); operates facilities for ICE, BOP, and state systems nationwide
companies: The GEO Group, Inc. (private prison company)

title: L.G.M.L. v. Noem (UAC emergency class action)
legal_provisions: TVPRA §§1232, 7105 (UAC protections); Fifth Amendment due process; APA
allegations: Trump administration sought to summarily deport Guatemalan UAC from ORR custody without required legal review, case-by-case trafficking screening, or notice — violating TVPRA mandatory protections for unaccompanied children
knowingly_benefit: TRO issued at 4:22 AM on day of filing (Aug 30, 2025): court blocked deportations
resolution: Court blocked summary deportation of unaccompanied children without TVPRA-required screening
legal_significance: Critically important for trafficking protection: TVPRA requires every UAC be screened for trafficking before deportation. Administration's deportation without screening = direct TVPRA violation.
specific_metrics: 76+ children at immediate risk of deportation on day of filing. HHS had 99.4% post-release service coverage as of start of FY2025; Mar 2025 contract cancellation for UAC legal services created protection vacuum.
summary: Critically important for trafficking protection: TVPRA requires every UAC be screened for trafficking before deportation. Administration's deportation without screening = direct TVPRA violation.
description: Trump administration sought to summarily deport Guatemalan UAC from ORR custody without required legal review, case-by-case trafficking screening, or notice — violating TVPRA mandatory protections for unaccompanied children Shows active litigation between TVPA obligations and immigration enforcement priorities in 2025

updates.event: Complaint filed — L.G.M.L. v. Noem (UAC emergency class action)
Status as of March 2026: Active — TRO issued; case continuing
updates.notes: Shows active litigation between TVPA obligations and immigration enforcement priorities in 2025

title: Garcia Ramirez v. ICE (UAC detention class action)
plaintiffs: Class of unaccompanied minors turning 18 in ORR custody
legal_provisions: TVPRA; Fifth Amendment due process; APA
allegations: ICE automatically transferred unaccompanied minors to adult detention the moment they turned 18, without case-by-case review, due process, or notice — eliminating TVPRA protections
knowingly_benefit: Permanent injunction issued 2021: ICE must provide individualized review and notice before transferring UAC to adult detention at age 18. Injunction ENFORCED against Trump administration December 12,
resolution: Permanent injunction: individualized review required before age-18 transfer to adult ICE detention
legal_significance: Court enforcement of permanent injunction against Trump administration in Dec 2025 = important constraint on administration's immigration enforcement affecting trafficking-vulnerable minors
specific_metrics: Class members: UAC who turn 18 in ORR custody. Without injunction, transfer to adult ICE facility eliminates all special protections and dramatically increases trafficking and abuse risk.
summary: Court enforcement of permanent injunction against Trump administration in Dec 2025 = important constraint on administration's immigration enforcement affecting trafficking-vulnerable minors
description: ICE automatically transferred unaccompanied minors to adult detention the moment they turned 18, without case-by-case review, due process, or notice — eliminating TVPRA protections Active enforcement against current administration; demonstrates judicial limits on executive immigration discretion where TVPA applies

updates.event: Complaint filed — Garcia Ramirez v. ICE (UAC detention class action)
Status as of March 2026: Concluded (injunction); enforcement ongoing 2025
updates.notes: Active enforcement against current administration; demonstrates judicial limits on executive immigration discretion where TVPA applies

title: TVPA Civil Litigation Aggregate - Annual Tracking
allegations: Total §1595 civil cases filed in federal courts by year; industry breakdown; outcome tracking
knowingly_benefit: 1,212 total §1595 cases filed through December 31, 2024; 280 filed in 2024 alone (highest ever); §1595 cases increased from 6 in 2004 to 280 in 2024 — 46-fold increase over 20 years
settlement_amount: Multiple settlements, verdicts, and dismissals across 1,212+ cases
survivor_compensation: $255M+ in civil damages and public settlements to survivors across all cases through 2024
legal_significance: 77% of federal civil trafficking cases include forced labor allegations. Hotel cases = largest category. Bank cases = largest per-case settlements.
specific_metrics: Specific metrics: 1,212 total cases (2004–2024); 280 in 2024; $255M+ in damages; 77% forced labor; sex trafficking = largest category by case count
